metadata.title: The Number Hierarchy — Every Number You Will Ever Need

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Merhaba, Liam — in for Bear. The number hierarchy — every number you will ever need? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.command: The number hierarchy — every number you will ever need?

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. The number hierarchy — every number you will ever need.

lines: Every number you will ever need

beats.narration_text: Every number fits inside a nested structure. Natural numbers live inside integers, which live inside rationals, which live inside reals, which live inside complex numbers. Knowing which set a number belongs to tells you which operations are safe.
claude — write a Manim scene showing the number set hierarchy: N subset Z subset Q subset R subset C as nested boxes with labeled examples of each set

props.command:
claude "write a Manim scene showing the number set hierarchy:
  N ⊂ Z ⊂ Q ⊂ R ⊂ C
  as nested boxes with labeled examples of each set"

beats.narration_text: The CLI writes it. number_sets.py — five nested boxes, left to right, with set symbols, names, and example members. Each box grows in turn. Arrows connect adjacent sets to show the subset relation.

props.code:
# number_sets.py — Claude Code output
SETS = [("ℕ","Natural","{1,2,3…}"),
        ("ℤ","Integer","{…−2,−1,0,1…}"),
        ("ℚ","Rational","{p/q}"),
        ("ℝ","Real","{+irrationals}"),
        ("ℂ","Complex","{a+bi}")]
# → draws 5 nested boxes, left to right, with arrows

beats.narration_text: Run it. Five boxes appear left to right — Natural, Integer, Rational, Real, Complex. Each box grows from center. Arrows connect adjacent sets. The subtitle confirms the total nesting: N subset Z subset Q subset R subset C.
claude — add one example number in each set ring showing exactly which rings it belongs to: use 3, negative 2, one half, pi, and square root of negative 1

props.command:
claude "add one example number in each set ring
  showing exactly which rings it belongs to:
  use 3, -2, 1/2, pi, and sqrt(-1)"

beats.narration_text: Five numbers placed in their smallest containing sets. 3 belongs to all five sets. Negative 2 enters at integers. One half enters at rationals. Pi enters at reals — irrational, not rational. Square root of negative one is complex only.

The nesting is total: every natural number is an integer, every integer is rational. The only direction that doesn't hold is down — pi is real but not rational, and there is no algebraic shortcut that makes it rational.
Pick any five numbers from a textbook problem set. Classify each into its smallest containing set. When a number is rational, verify it — write it as p over q explicitly.

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Here's what the body just demonstrated. The nesting is total: every natural number is an integer, every integer is rational. The only direction that doesn't hold is down — pi is real but not rational, and there is no algebraic shortcut that makes it rational. Pick any five numbers from a textbook problem set. Classify each into its smallest containing set. When a number is rational, verify it — write it as p over q explicitly.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [The Number Hierarchy — Every Number You Will Ever Need] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
